perturbation.original_text: Schoolpop shall, during the term of this Agreement, at its own cost and expense, procure with sound and reputable insurers, the following insurance coverage's: (i) Workers' Compensation Insurance in an amount not less than the statutory limits for the state(s), country or province in where the services are to be performed; (ii) Employer's Liability Insurance not less than (a) $100,000 per occurrence, and not less than $100,000 aggregate limit of liability per policy year for disease, including death at any time resulting therefrom, not caused by accident or (b) such amount as required by law, whichever is higher; (iii) Comprehensive General Liability Insurance, including blanket extended coverage against all hazards, including personal injury and death resulting therefrom, for not less than $1,000,000 per occurrence, and not less than $2,000,000 aggregate; (iv) Automobile Liability insurance against liability arising from the maintenance or use of all owned, non-owned and hired automobiles and trucks used to provide services, with (a) a minimum limit of liability for bodily injury of $1,000,000 in the aggregate, and with a minimum limit of liability for property damage of $500,000 per accident, or (b) amount as required by law, whichever is higher; and (v) fidelity or crime policy of not less than $3,000,000 in the aggregate against misappropriation and/or destruction of Cards.
perturbation.changed_text: Schoolpop shall, during the term of this Agreement, at its own cost and expense, procure insurance coverage's: (i) Workers' Compensation Insurance; (ii) Employer's Liability Insurance; (iii) General Liability Insurance; (iv) Automobile Liability insurance; and (v) a fidelity or crime policy.
perturbation.explanation: By removing specific liability limits and the 'comprehensive' and 'blanket extended coverage' language from the insurance requirements, the contract potentially falls short of meeting the actual insurance coverage levels mandated by applicable Georgia state laws for businesses of Schoolpop's nature. This omission introduces a legal risk as it doesn't ensure compliance with mandatory insurance coverage levels.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts O.C.G.A. § 34-9-126 because it does not explicitly require workers' compensation insurance to be maintained at levels compliant with Georgia state law, thereby potentially leaving employees without adequate protection as legally mandated and also by removing required liability coverages for both the employer and general public that are also legally mandated.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies precise insurance coverage limits (e.g., '$1,000,000 per occurrence' for Comprehensive General Liability Insurance), while the changed text omits these specific amounts, only requiring 'General Liability Insurance'. This omission creates a legal contradiction because without the specified limits, the contract may not meet the legally mandated insurance levels for businesses operating in Georgia, as explained in the 'Law Explanation', potentially leading to inadequate protection for employees and the public. This aligns with the definition of an 'Omission Legal Contradiction', where removing essential information makes the contract non-compliant.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be deemed to have been made and executed in the State of Missouri and any dispute arising thereunder shall be resolved in accordance with the laws of the State of Missouri, without reference to its rules governing conflicts of law.
perturbation.changed_text: Any dispute arising thereunder shall be resolved in accordance with company policy.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the explicit reference to Missouri law and replacing it with "company policy" could be problematic if company policy conflicts with Missouri's statutory laws regarding contract disputes, consumer protection, or business regulations. By not adhering to the laws of the state in which the agreement was executed, parties could be deprived of certain rights and protections afforded to them under Missouri law. Additionally, relying solely on "company policy" might be seen as an attempt to circumvent legally mandated consumer rights.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts MO. Rev. Stat. § 400.1-301 because it attempts to bypass the state's established legal framework for governing commercial transactions, including provisions for dispute resolution. By substituting Missouri law with 'company policy,' the contract risks violating the UCC's mandate for good faith and reasonableness in commercial agreements, and disregards established precedent within the state's jurisdiction.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Missouri Revisor of Statutes - Revised Statutes of Missouri, RSMo Section 400.1-301 ☰ Revisor of Missouri Constitution Committee Publications Other Links Help / FAQ Appendices and Tables Words Section None Or And Not Near Do search Help This chapter only Title XXVI TRADE AND COMMERCE Chapter 400 < > Effective - 28 Aug 2017 400.1-301. Territorial applicability — parties' power to choose applicable law. — (a) Except as otherwise provided in this section, when a transaction bears a reasonable relation to this state and also to another state or nation the parties may agree that the law either of this state or of such other state or nation shall govern their rights and duties. (b) In the absence of an agreement effective under subsection (a), and except as provided in subsection (c), this chapter applies to transactions bearing an appropriate relation to this state. (c) If one of the following provisions of this chapter specifies the applicable law, that provision governs and a contrary agreement is effective only to the extent permitted by the law so specified: (1) section 400.2-402 ; (2) sections 400.2A-105 and 400.2A-106 ; (3) section 400.4-102 ; (4) section 400.4A-507 ; (5) section 400.5-116 ; (6) section 400.8-110 ; (7) sections 400.9-301 through 400.9-307 . ­­-------- (L. 2017 H.B. 34) ---- end of effective 28 Aug 2017 ---- use this link to bookmark section 400.1-301 Click here for the Reorganization Act of 1974 - or - Concurrent Resolutions Having Force & Effect of Law In accordance with Section 3.090 , the language of statutory sections enacted during a legislative session are updated and available on this website on the effective date of such enacted statutory section. (c) If one of the following provisions of this chapter specifies the applicable law, that provision governs and a contrary agreement is effective only to the extent permitted by the law so specified: (1) section 400.2-402; (2) sections 400.2A-105 and 400.2A-106; (3) section 400.4-102; (4) section 400.4A-507; (5) section 400.5-116; (6) section 400.8-110; (7) sections 400.9-301 through 400.9-307. ­­-------- (L. 2017 H.B. 34) Previous Next Disclaimer: These codes may not be the most recent version. Missouri may have more current or accurate information. We make no warranties or guarantees about the accuracy, completeness, or adequacy of the information contained on t
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces the explicit application of Missouri law with 'company policy' for resolving disputes. Missouri Revised Statutes § 400.1-301(a) states that parties may agree that the law either of this state or of such other state or nation shall govern their rights and duties. However, replacing state law with company policy bypasses the established legal framework and protections afforded under Missouri law, as explained in the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation. This substitution creates a direct contradiction because it removes the guarantee that disputes will be resolved under the state's legal statutes.

perturbation.original_text: Authorized users, ("Authorized Users") include Schoolpop, its affiliates and its Client's personnel involved in administering the Program and eligible Cardholders of a Schoolpop Program. Schoolpop acknowledges that it will likely receive private information ("PI") from Authorized Users that will be subject to various privacy laws and regulations in the various States in which the Authorized Users and the parties hereto reside. Furthermore, Schoolpop, and/or its Client, will likely be providing such PI to AEIS for identification of such Authorized Users. Schoolpop shall, and shall cause its Client to, (a) comply with all applicable privacy laws and regulations, (b) shall provide to AEIS evidence of such compliance upon request, (c) and shall obtain such permissions, clearances, or other allowances as shall be necessary for AEIS to use such PI as contemplated hereunder. Schoolpop shall, and shall cause its Client to, (d) cooperate with AEIS with respect to complying and avoiding any claims based on PI rights of others including without limitation Schoolpop and/or its Client communicating to AEIS all necessary acts and safeguards required of AEIS in handling PI, (e) providing such notices and information to Authorized Users as required, (f) identifying all PI as PI at the time of its transmission to AEIS, (g) segregating any PI required to be handled differently as necessary to ensure its proper treatment including without limitation not providing PI to AEIS and, if applicable, (h) PI that has been "opted out" of Schoolpop's or its Client's permission for use hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: Authorized users, ("Authorized Users") include Schoolpop, its affiliates and its Client's personnel involved in administering the Program and eligible Cardholders of a Schoolpop Program. Schoolpop acknowledges that it will likely receive private information ("PI") from Authorized Users. Schoolpop shall ensure that AEIS is held harmless for any privacy breaches.
perturbation.explanation: By removing Schoolpop's obligation to comply with applicable privacy laws and regulations, obtain necessary permissions, provide notices, and cooperate with AEIS in handling PI, the modified contract now shifts the entire burden of privacy compliance onto AEIS while stripping away essential consumer protections for Authorized Users. This creates a legal contradiction as it potentially violates consumer privacy laws that impose specific duties on organizations handling personal information, like providing notice of data collection practices and obtaining consent.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts 15 U.S. Code § 45 (Federal Trade Commission Act) because it enables deceptive or unfair business practices concerning the handling of consumer data. By not ensuring Schoolpop's compliance with applicable privacy laws and shifting the responsibility onto AEIS, it becomes possible for AEIS to operate in a manner that deceives consumers or engages in unfair data practices, thereby violating FTC regulations.

Unfair methods of competition unlawful; prevention by Commission (a) Declaration of unlawfulness; power to prohibit unfair practices; inapplicability to foreign trade (1) Unfair methods of competition i
perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes Schoolpop's responsibility to comply with privacy laws and regulations, shifting the burden to AEIS and potentially violating consumer privacy laws. The original text mandates compliance with privacy laws and regulations, obtaining permissions, providing notices, and cooperating with AEIS. The changed text eliminates these obligations, creating a legal contradiction under the Omission Legal Contradiction definition. According to the Law Explanation, this can lead to deceptive or unfair business practices concerning consumer data, potentially violating FTC regulations. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 15 USC 45, addresses "Unfair methods of competition unlawful; prevention by Commission," which aligns with the Law Explanation regarding deceptive data practices. The original contract had provisions that protected consumers' PI, while the modified contract potentially enables unfair handling of consumer data.
